sentences.text: Skeletal.
I think funnily enough, I didn't see many women.
I think I mainly only saw men.
There were some women there, but I think sometimes very hard to distinguish.
You know, they're wobbling rags, literally.
I must say they were piles of the dead there, piles dead, piles of half living.
We went in some of the huts, all wooden huts there and some were still in their wooden beds.
You know.
But I suppose you could call it communal beds that they had there.
They were half dead, limber half dead.
[SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A very, very-- you know, you just can't believe what you saw and I thought to myself there must be hundreds of these camps around Germany which we knew about.
And I think myself and this other Jewish friend of mine, David Goldstein, I think we were the only Jewish-- two Jewish soldiers there-- there that went into it with that field regiment.
And I mean, I saw some English soldiers break down and cry.
I cried, you know, with what we saw.
I can't believe it, sat down and cried.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We gave assistance to the ones that we-- that were still alive, gave them biscuits, not in big quantities.
We all knew not to feed them too much because, you know, the condition they were in.
A lot of them were taken by ambulance, I think to the field hospitals were remaining there.
My job, unfortunately, was bulldozing the corpses into these big pits.
Yeah.
Well, you didn't.
You bulldozed them which was pretty awful.
I had to wear a mask over my face, the stench.
Well, the ones that were alive or barely alive, they took out in field ambulance.
I think they probably took them to Belsen, the town itself, put them in the hospitals.
I don't know.
A lot of them were sort of lifted out, taken away from the camp, you know, treatment.
Well, British soldiers and Germans, Germans that were there.
They were made to do it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think a lot of German civilians were brought into the camp, I recollect, brought to a camp.
To help taking out the mere-life prisoners, you know, onto ambulances and onto trucks to move them to-- by the way, there was a local hospital they just made facilities for these people.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
The Germans?
No.
No.
I didn't speak to them at all.
I just saw--
I think I looked at them with bloody hatred.
I'll be quite honest, you know?
No, I didn't speak to any German.
I didn't want to.
I didn't feel like it.
And we were kept away from them, actually, you know.
The command-- command officers there just didn't allow us to contact them either.
Yeah.
I spoke to a few of them that was still laying on the ground, not inside the huts.
They were lying on the ground.
Come?
Well, I said to one of them who very, very frailly was covered with a plaid coat.
[INAUDIBLE] a blanket and I heard him say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]?
And he picked up my hand and kissed me on the hand.
And I-- I said to him, you'll be all right.
You know.
And funnily enough, I think they were all Jews in Belsen.
I came across, I think, quite a few gypsies and non-Jews.
They were also prisoners there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I tell you what, I don't think I could have begged to stay there longer than I did.
I think I was ready to run away about two or three days.
A think two days.
No.
We doing-- we were clearing up as well.
We were helping, giving assistance where we could.
No, I didn't see any children.
Can't remember seeing any children.
I think mainly adults I remember seeing there.
Don't remember seeing any children, unless they-- they were there and they'd been moved out by the Germans.
I don't know unless they'd already been moved out prior to our get-- getting into the camp.
No.
I only went into one or two of the huts there.
I didn't see any.
I think I recollect seeing one.
In one of the huts in the far corner, some sort of toilet which smelled bloody horrible.
I think that toilet was for all the inmates of that particular hut.
I remember that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No, I doubt think there was any sanitation there at all.
I think they had to do the initial things out in the open or even on the floors of the hut, I don't know.
They were wood.
They're [? wood, well, I ?] remember them.
They were tiered inside.
You know.
I didn't-- I can't-- I didn't see any.
I didn't see any at all.
I saw straw, a lot of straw there.
Oh, yeah.
By the British Army, and the-- was preparing food better, and I think they were very careful the way they supplied it.
You know.
We were told not to over feed them.
You know, to look after them.
Then-- then it wasn't my job in any case.
That was a-- I think the-- what was it, K3 Corps?
Can't remember.
But food was brought into the camp, you know, to feed them soup or things like that.
I can't recollect, Susie.
I can't recollect.
It's gonna be too far back.
It's only certain things that stand in my mind.
I probably didn't attach any importance to that kind of thing at the time.
No.
No, we didn't see it.
I didn't see it.
I think there was some ovens there.
I think there was because I remembered before we entered the camp, there was a lot of smoke.
Yeah, well I smelled dead bodies and stench and I mean, the place smelled.
Didn't see any.
Can't remember.
I didn't see any.
I didn't see any.
Yeah, I think the numbers on the huts, but that's about all I know.
I can't-- can't remember.
Well, the only one I remember seeing was Josef Kramer.
He was a camp commandant there.
Yeah, Irma Grese came.
She was in charge of the women's quarters, I think.
I don't think they had any strength in them to show any reaction.
I think they were just pleased they were liberated, freed.
You know.
I don't think they had the strength to show any-- anything, any emotion.
You know.
I would say they were more like zombies.
What-- what reaction could a zombie give?
The burial?
No, I was just using the bulldozer to bulldoze these bodies that were lying around into the pits.
Oh, yeah, mass graves.
Oh, yeah.
[? 1,000, ?] they were all buried together.
That's something I-- I just can't remember.
How do you distinguish a Jew from a gypsy in a concentration camp?
No.
No, just mass grave.
Just big pits.
No.
I think it was probably all mixed up.
[INAUDIBLE] I think, you know, on hindsight I think Belsen was mainly a contraction camp for Jewish people, Jewish inmates, but we were told no, that they were other than Jewish inmates in that camp.
So with that being moved from other camps prior to capturing Belsen, I don't know, which is quite possible.
You know Germans probably shifted other inmates into there, but I always had the thought that Belsen was mainly a Jewish concentration camp, but I don't think so.
No, we were in camps and I think for one day we were in a-- in a hotel building in Belsen itself, not in the camp, but the little town of Belsen.
I think they'd all been evacuated.
No, I think I met probably two Germans, but I don't know who they were.
I'm sure they did.
I'm sure they did.
Because apparently after I left Belsen, I was told that they got the whole German population what was left into the camps to see what they had done.
And I think even the mayor of Belsen, the Bergermeister, but that was after-- but we heard that afterwards, you know?
They couldn't believe it.
They couldn't believe that Nazis, the German party could do anything like that.
But you know, who knows.
Bull.
You know what I mean by bull?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, obviously, they were German Jews.
I think they was quite also from Holland and also I think from Germany itself.
Yeah.
Germans.
No, but I could tell you.
Five, three-- probably about 23,000, I think.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know one-- one grave held 5,000 and another one held 3,000.
I don't even know.
I don't even know, Susie.
You know.
Oh, yeah.
There was a Jewish chaplain.
I think there was a Jewish army chaplain there, yeah.
Can't remember it.
No.
No.
I'm pretty certain I remember seeing a Jewish army chaplain there.
Oh, I-- we were only there for three days, I believe, three or four-- I think three days at most, then we moved on.
Well, I suppose another field regiment might have come in.
Another branch of British Army might have come in.
I don't know.
But I think it took about 10 days to clean and clear the camp.
I think was put to torch the day I left with flame throwers.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think it was one or two months after the war finished even.
That I'm not certain about.
Oh, yeah.
We were told it was put to torch, cleaned up, and made a monument of the place.
I know that, yeah, made a memorial to the place.
Well, evacuation of the--
Of the inmates?
Yeah.
I don't know, could have been just being evacuated to Belsen itself-- another town itself or some other place I-- you know, that was already captured in Germany.
You know.
I don't think they could have been very, very far because they were too ill-- too weak, too ailing.
You know.
Oh yeah.
Oh, yeah.
I think that was the purpose of the-- you know, but I'm sure of it.
You know, I mean, I saw quite a number of them taken by stretcher onto the ambulances that were there and the trucks that were there.
You know.
And where they were going to, I'm not quite certain, but they must be taken to a place where they could be suckled, saved, fed, clothed.
Horror.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I shouldn't really say this, but I said to myself, there's no damned God.
I became an atheist after that.
I just didn't want to believe there was a God.
I couldn't believe it.
Pardon?
Well, especially my friend, David Goldstein.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He felt the same way as me.
[INAUDIBLE] some of the British soldiers me, some of my friends in the regiment with me.
They sat down and cried like I did when we first saw it.
You know, they-- they weren't Jewish and when they say it, they say down and cried.
You know.
They didn't cry, they wept.
If they didn't wept, they sobbed.
You know.
Pardon?
Bulldozing the corpses into the graves.
That's something I'll never forget.
What could I say?
Just mass of bodies.
You know, you're pushing dead bodies with a big blade into these mass graves.
You know.
Pardon?
No, most of them were naked.
Some had tatters, you know, dressed in tatters, but I suppose they died on the spot.
They used to drop dead in the camp.
They just didn't take any notice of them.
You know.
I didn't want to do it.
As a matter of fact, after one day, I said to my [INAUDIBLE], I said, I just don't want to carry on doing this.
He said, I understand.
He said, I understand.
We had to take relays.
You just couldn't do the whole time.
It's just, [INAUDIBLE] nauseating.
It's just you became-- you became dehumanized to think that you could-- you know, that I was shoveling Jewish bodies into a grave.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Definitely.
More than angry.
I was-- I was angry with God.
[CRYING]
Yeah.
Yeah.
I only know I'm Jewish because I'm Jewish, but as far as him up there, I don't know.
Of course, I was.
Of course, I was.
How else could you feel?
You know, you think, how could people-- you know, how could they do a thing like that?
You know?
Especially also knowing, I suppose, because you know it wasn't only Jews.

It some of their own people, you know, they did it to, lots of German, non-Jewish people were put into these camps as well just because they didn't side with the Nazi philosophy, you know, or they were socialists or they were communists or they were gypsies.
Where, in Germany?
Oh, yeah.
I think we heard quite a bit.
But I think a lot of it did happen, not-- maybe not necessarily in Germany, but in places like Czechoslovakia and France where they had massacres-- massacred.

There was a place called Oradour-sur-Glane where the Germans [INAUDIBLE] executed a whole village and also like in Czechoslovakia in Lidice where they slaughtered a whole village, so you heard about these things.
After.
After.
Yeah.
I met American soldiers, and I met Polish soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't see many French soldiers.
They were mainly Maquis-- the Maquis.
Yeah, I met Polish soldiers.
I met American soldiers and then French soldiers I didn't meet, [LAUGHS] very strange.
Allied prison of war.
No, I don't recollect.
I don't recollect.
Anything I recollect, they weren't Allied, but German slave workers who were being shipped back to their own countries, I saw that.
That I don't know, but I should think like France, Germany-- France, Poland, Russia.
They love Russian slave workers that we knew about, Polish slave workers.
Yeah, I went to-- we went on to Magdeburg-- was it Magdeburg?
Braunschweig, Hannover.
What I can't remember, be quiet, Susie.
I can't remember if I went to Belsen from Hannover or I went from Brunswick, that I can't remember, but it's either those two.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, the British Army occupation around there, yeah.
Policing, I suppose, You could call it policing.
Or--
Keeping the population, you know, under control.
Repairing damaged buildings that were being used by the British.
And policing occupation army.
It was called British Army Occupation of the Rhine.
There's a tissue.
No, that's all right.
It's just known as B-- BAOR.
In Berlin?
From 1945 right till my discharge, 1946.
Oh, yeah.
No.
No, not at all.
No.
Yeah.
Funnily enough in Hannover, a Jewish hospitality committee opened a canteen there.
The Jewish-- it was called the Jewish Hospitality Committee.
Now, I don't know if it was English.
I don't know if it was German or what, but they opened up this canteen in Hannover.
That's something I'll never forget.
I had my first taste of lokshen soup there.
Yeah.
[LAUGHS]
What, Jewish?
No, not whilst I was in Berlin.
Not whilst I was in Berlin, no.
No.
We went by train.
I went from Berlin to Hameln, which is called commonly Hamelin where the piped piper came from.
From there, I went by train because all the railways were all disrupted there, so you had to go through-- go in certain lines to get to your destination.
From England, we went by boat, naturally, but from Berlin we've been to a place called Hameln-- [INAUDIBLE].
And we went on from there to Tournai, which was in Belgium, and from there we got on a boat to England.
Went to bed with my wife.
[LAUGHS] No.
I'm joking, Don't put that in, David.
Cut that out.
What did I do?

Well, number one, we went to a disembarkation station where we handed in our uniforms and we were given civilian clothes by the-- by the British government, which I think is the only country in the world that did.
Gave you a suit, shirt, shoes, tie, and a hat, a trilby hat.
And you had to hand in your uniform.
Because after that, I still had five years to do in the army as a reservist.
But anyway I was never called up after that.
No, wait.
Where did I land?
I went to Tournai to Southampton, from Southampton, by train to London.
Most of that I can't remember.
I think it was Waterloo Station.
Yeah.
No.
She had two kids.
No, I told her I was coming home anyway.
Oh, yeah.
By letter when I got to England.
We didn't have a phone then, so that's not true.
No, we couldn't afford a phone then.
No, by letter, I think mainly.
Yeah.
'46, yeah.
Well, I had seven days leave.
I-- you know, they gave me leave from the army.
I got a-- I think it was seven days.
When I took my army book there.
I think I had seven to 14 days leave, which I spent with my wife and my two kids.
My youngest child hadn't been born yet.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was suppose we're all happy war was over.
Oh, yeah.
Well, she went through the whole blitz, my wife did.
Got bombed out twice.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Food was short but not scarce.
You know, they gave you a sufficient ration to be able to live on.
Pardon?
When?
After I came?
Well, I had seven days' leave then I went to work-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] this-- this I'm gonna have to remember now.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I did do some work.
I don't know.
I did jobs as a radio mechanic, I think, a firm I used to work for before the war.
That's right, yeah.
I was fixing up aerials.
Mm.
Well, I started up my own business after time.
I went into business on my own.
Yeah.
And well, I buy and sell government surplus material, you know, right up to the time I came here to Australia.
What made me come to Australia?
Well, my-- my eldest son, David, who was about 17 at the time-- this was 19-- decided to come to Australia with a friend.
He wanted to try his luck here.
I think that was 19-- 1967.
Yeah, I think it was about 1965, he came to Australia.
And then we got a letter from him to say he'd met a Jewish girl, and he was gonna get married.
So my wife and I decided to come here for his wedding with my young son, Barry.
Came here for the wedding.
Stayed here a think for about three or four weeks.

And I think we fell in love with Australia and decided to come back, so it took me a couple of years, you know, to sell the business I had there, and we came back here in 1969 and right up to the present day.
Unfortunately, my wife had died in '91-- '91, yeah.
But life has got to go on.
Yeah.
Well, I still light Shabbos candles.
I still pray to God.
Right?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I feel, I still feel quite to the present day I can't believe there's a God.
Life is wonderful if you're fit and well and healthy and rich.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yeah.
I mean, obviously, they've seen photos, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but they know that-- that I-- you know, I was in the army for that time.
So obviously, they know I had to experience something which--
Which--
OK.
